content: A large language model (LLM) generates text by leveraging a deep neural network architecture, typically a transformer, trained on vast corpora of text to learn statistical patterns of language. During generation, the model processes an input prompt by encoding contextual information into high-dimensional embeddings. It then predicts subsequent tokens autoregressively, selecting the next word based on the conditional probability distribution derived from prior tokens and learned language representations. This prediction is refined through multi-head self-attention mechanisms that capture dependencies across different positions in the sequence, enabling nuanced understanding of syntax, semantics, and pragmatics. The model’s parameters, optimized via gradient descent on cross-entropy loss during pretraining, implicitly encode linguistic, factual, and world knowledge. Sampling strategies—such as greedy decoding, beam search, or nucleus sampling—govern the trade-off between coherence and variability in output. Hence, text generation emerges not from explicit rules but from probabilistic inference over a richly encoded latent space shaped by extensive exposure to natural language data.
